coredata.dc:description: Visible light driven catalytic hydrogen production over CdS based catalysts could be a good candidate strategy to help solve the global energy crisis. To improve the photocatalytic H2 production activity of CdS, the series of Pd nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) co-modified CdS nanorods (NRs) composites were designed to simultaneously build the type I heterojunctions and Schottky junctions. The photocatalytic H2 evolution activities of the samples were assessed under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation and the reaction mechanism was proposed. The Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS catalyst exhibited excellent activity with the average hydrogen evolution rate (HER) of 47.1 mmol h−1 g−1, which is 36.2 times higher than that of the pure CdS. Its apparent quantum efficiency is 17.1% (450 nm light). The combined action of Schottky junctions and type I heterojunctions in the composite Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS greatly promoted charge transfer and separation, resulting in enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the synergistic effect of the Pd nanoparticles and CQDs resulted in enhanced visible light absorption and optimized band structure, helpful to enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. This study provides a simple and cost-effective strategy for improving visible light catalytic H2 production performance of catalysts.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant no. 22275140] and Tianjin municipal Science and Technology Commission [grant no. 20YDTPJC00650].

The as-synthesized catalysts were characterized by XRD patterns, SEM and TEM images, XPS, EDS spectra and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms to get information on their crystal structures, morphologies, compositions and BET surface areas. UV\u2013vis diffuse reflectance spectra, photoluminescence spectra, transient photocurrent curves, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (M \u2212 S) plots were measured to explore the photoelectric properties and energy band potentials of the samples. The photocatalytic H2 production performances of the series of CdS-based samples were investigated under visible light irradiation (\u03BB \u2265 420 nm) using the 0.25 M Na2SO3-0.35 M Na2S sacrificial agent. The detailed descriptions were provided in the electronic supporting information (ESI).The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Lianjie Zhu reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Lianjie Zhu reports financial support was provided by Tianjin Municipal Science and Technology Commission. Lianjie Zhu reports a relationship with Tianjin University of Technology that includes: employment. Other authors have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.We thank the supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant no. 22275140] and Tianjin municipal Science and Technology Commission [grant no. 20YDTPJC00650].

head.ce:title: Pd-CQDs/CdS ternary composite for highly efficient visible light driven H2 evolution under combined action of type I heterojunctions and Schottky junctions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visible light driven catalytic hydrogen production over CdS based catalysts could be a good candidate strategy to help solve the global energy crisis. To improve the photocatalytic H2 production activity of CdS, the series of Pd nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) co-modified CdS nanorods (NRs) composites were designed to simultaneously build the type I heterojunctions and Schottky junctions. The photocatalytic H2 evolution activities of the samples were assessed under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation and the reaction mechanism was proposed. The Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS catalyst exhibited excellent activity with the average hydrogen evolution rate (HER) of 47.1 mmol h−1 g−1, which is 36.2 times higher than that of the pure CdS. Its apparent quantum efficiency is 17.1% (450 nm light). The combined action of Schottky junctions and type I heterojunctions in the composite Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS greatly promoted charge transfer and separation, resulting in enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the synergistic effect of the Pd nanoparticles and CQDs resulted in enhanced visible light absorption and optimized band structure, helpful to enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. This study provides a simple and cost-effective strategy for improving visible light catalytic H2 production performance of catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: The over exploitation of fossil fuels led to severe environmental pollution, excessive greenhouse gas emissions and global warming [1]. Thus, it is urgent to develop renewable and environmentally friendly clean energy sources [2–4]. Hydrogen is a clean energy source with high energy density and solar energy is an attractive renewable energy source to facilitate H2 production by water splitting [5–12]. Photocatalytic H2 production technology is a green technology that utilizes semiconductor materials to catalyze water splitting reactions under light irradiation [13–16]. Particularly, visible light photocatalysis is greatly promising and forward-looking technology due to high utilization of sunlight [17]. While CdS nanomaterials are excellent candidate photocatalysts owing to their suitable band gap (∼2.4 eV, enabling high response to visible light) [18,19] and favorable energy band positions (meeting the potential requirement for water splitting, ∼1.23 eV), compared to the wide band gap semiconductors, such as TiO2, ZnO and ZnS [20–22]. However, the pure phase CdS displays poor photocatalytic performance of H2 production, which is mainly caused by rapid recombination of the photogenerated electrons and holes [23–27]. Besides, the weak absorption of the pure CdS to longer wavelength of visible light could also impede its photocatalytic performance, limiting its practical applications.

Loading small amount of noble metal co-catalysts onto the CdS nanomaterial is an efficient method to promote electron-hole transfer/separation and enhance visible light absorption in longer wavelength region, leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity [28–30]. Metallic Pd was found a cost-effective co-catalyst which generally functions as an electron trap and active center provider [31–35]. For instance, Güy et al. designed a Pd–CdS/g-C3N4 photocatalyst, in which CdS and g-C3N4 form a type II heterojunction structure incorporated with Pd nanoparticles [36]. The synergistic effect in this catalyst increased separation of photogenerated electrons-holes, accelerated interfacial charge transfer and enhanced visible light absorption, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic hydrogen production. Although Pd is much cheaper than Pt, it is after all a noble metal with high price. Therefore, how to maximize the advantage of a small amount of Pd co-catalyst is still a challenge task. While introducing other low-cost co-catalyst and let it synergistic act with Pd is a good strategy.

Numerous researchers have demonstrated that non-metal carbon nanomaterials as co-catalysts can effectively promote photocatalytic H2 evolution [37,38]. Currently, the main non-metal carbon nanomaterials include graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [39–42]. These materials not only provide high surface areas and many reactive sites but also enhance visible light absorption, further stimulating interfacial charge transfer [43]. Therefore, selecting a low-cost, non-toxic, highly efficient and stable inorganic carbon nanomaterial as a co-catalyst for the Pd/CdS composite is of significant importance [44]. The low cost CQDs, discovered in 2004, possess particle sizes less than 10 nm and high conductivity, which make them a good co-catalyst. For instance, Manchala et al. successfully synthesized the ternary composite CQDs/LaFeO3/CdS which displayed enhanced hydrogen production efficiency under sunlight, 2.53 mmol h−1 g−1, twice that of the pure CdS [45]. The incorporation of CQDs effectively enhanced visible light absorption and improved interfacial charge separation. Therefore, here, CQDs and Pd were chosen as two co-catalysts for synthesis of CdS composites.

In this work, the ternary composite materials, Pd-CQDs/CdS, were synthesized for the first time and characterized, featuring dual co-catalysts of Pd nanoparticles and CQDs loaded onto the CdS NRs. Their photocatalytic hydrogen production activity, stability and mechanism were investigated. The Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS catalyst exhibited excellent visible light catalytic H2 production activity, mainly attributed to the combined action of type I heterojunctions and Schottky junctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdS NRs, CQDs dispersion and composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CdS NRs were synthesized by a facile solvothermal method, referring to the literature [46], where 1.14 g of cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O), 2.28 g of thiourea (NH2CSNH2, ≥98.0%) and 40 mL of ethylenediamine (EDA) were used as the raw materials which underwent heating in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 140 °C for 36 h. The yellow CdS NRs were obtained after centrifugation, washing by ethanol and high-purity water until the solution's pH approaches neutrality and drying overnight in an oven. The CQD dispersion was prepared using a simple hydrothermal method. Initially, the easily obtainable poplar leaves were soaked in high-purity water overnight, washed and cut into small pieces. Subsequently, 10 g of these leaves were mixed with 40 mL of high-purity water in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h to obtain a brownish CQD dispersion with the concentration of 0.607 mol L−1 (measured by an evaporation-weighing method, referring to our previous work [20]). Filtration method was used for purifying the CQDs dispersion.

The CQDs/CdS composites were prepared by hydrothermal method, where 0.2 g of CdS powder, 0.5 mL of CQDs dispersion and 80 mL of high-purity water were used. The reaction mixture was stirred for 60 min, followed by sonication for 10 min, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C in an oven for 4 h. After ordinary centrifugation, washing and drying process, the product was collected. By varying the volume of CQDs dispersion (0.2 mL, 0.35 mL, 0.5 mL, 0.65 mL, 0.8 mL and 2 mL), series of CQDs/CdS composites with various CQDs contents are obtained. These samples were designated as 0.2CQDs/CdS, 0.35CQDs/CdS, 0.5CQDs/CdS, 0.65CQDs/CdS, 0.8CQDs/CdS and 2CQDs/CdS, respectively.

The Pd nanoparticles were deposited on the CdS surface by a facile, highly efficient and no impurity introduced photodeposition method to obtain the Pd-CQDs/CdS ternary composites. Firstly, 0.1 g of xCQDs/CdS (x = 0.2, 0.35, 0.5, 0.65, 0.8 or 2) powder was well dispersed in 90 mL of high-purity water by stirring for 30 min. Then 10 mL of methanol was added into the above mixture, followed by sonication for 10 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of PdCl2 solution (10 g L−1) was added dropwisely into the reaction mixture and stirred for another 10 min. Finally, the suspension was under irradiation by a 300 W xenon lamp for 1 h to facilitate the photodeposition of Pd nanoparticles onto the CQDs/CdS. After drying the mixture at 60 °C in an oven overnight, grinding the powder to obtain the Pd-CQDs/CdS composite. The as-obtained samples were called as Pd-xCQDs/CdS (x = 0.2, 0.35, 0.5, 0.65, 0.8 or 2). By varying the volume of PdCl2 solution (50, 75 or 150 μL), series of y%Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composites with various Pd contents were obtained, which were specified as the 0.5%Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS, 0.75%Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS and 1.5%Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS samples. Similarly, the Pd/CdS composite was prepared by photodeposition method using 100 μL of PdCl2 solution and 0.1 g of CdS powder. The synthesis process of the ternary composites was summarized in Scheme 1 . One notices that the adopted synthetic methods (hydro/solvothermal syntheses, sonication and photodeposition) are facile and no expensive/complicated apparatus was used, making it feasible for scalability of the synthesis process for industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations, photoelectric properties and photocatalytic tests
ce:section.ce:para: The as-synthesized catalysts were characterized by XRD patterns, SEM and TEM images, XPS, EDS spectra and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms to get information on their crystal structures, morphologies, compositions and BET surface areas. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, photoluminescence spectra, transient photocurrent curves, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots were measured to explore the photoelectric properties and energy band potentials of the samples. The photocatalytic H2 production performances of the series of CdS-based samples were investigated under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) using the 0.25 M Na2SO3-0.35 M Na2S sacrificial agent. The detailed descriptions were provided in the electronic supporting information (ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, morphology and composition of the products
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the crystal structures and compositions of the as-prepared samples, XRD patterns were conducted. As depicted in Fig. 1 , all the diffraction peaks of the pure CdS, 0.35CQDs/CdS and series of Pd-x%CQDs/CdS (x = 0, 0.2, 0.35 and 0.5) composites were indexed to the hexagonal phase of wurtzite-type CdS (JCPDS: 41–1049). The sharp and narrow diffraction peaks of the CdS-based samples indicate them well crystallized. After loading the Pd nanoparticles and CQDs onto the surface of CdS NRs, no new diffraction peaks were present, possibly due to the low contents or poor crystallinity of the CQDs and Pd. The introduction of CQDs and Pd did not affect the crystal structure of the CdS NRs.

The morphologies of the as-prepared CdS and its composites were investigated by the SEM images. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the pure CdS sample exhibits well-dispersed NRs with smooth surfaces. The lengths of the rods range from 200 to 400 nm and the diameters are about 20 nm. Upon loading of the CQDs or/and Pd nanoparticles, the rod-like structure of the CdS was preserved, but its surface became somewhat rough and aggregation occurred to certain extent. The Pd and/or CQDs particles were not observed clearly in the SEM images of the composites, likely due to their small sizes and low proportions.

To prove successful loading of the CQDs and Pd in the ternary composite, the STEM and element mapping images of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS sample were shown in Fig. 3 a-e. The results display that the elements Cd and S exhibit well-distributed rod-like stacking patterns, attributed to the NR structure of CdS. The Pd and C elements uniformly cover the NRs, confirming successful loading of Pd and CQDs on the CdS NRs homogeneously. The elemental compositions of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS sample were analyzed by the EDS spectrum (Fig. 3f). The result shows that the elements Cd (50.77%) and S (47.07%) constitute the majority of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS sample. Only small amount of Pd (1.02%) and C (1.14%) were present on the CdS surface. The relatively low contents of Pd and CQDs could explain the absence of their XRD peaks in Fig. 1.

Since the Pd nanoparticles and CQDs can't be observed clearly in the SEM images, the TEM and HRTEM images of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite were taken to observe the microstructures. Fig. 4 A depicts the NR morphology of the CdS with small sizes of nanoparticles uniformly distributed on the surface of the NRs. This can be observed more clearly in the magnified TEM image (Fig. 4B) for the marked region in Fig. 4A. The particle sizes of the Pd nanoparticles (marked by red dashed circles) are about 6–10 nm, while the CQDs (marked by blue dashed circles) are even smaller, about 4 nm, which were proved by the HRTEM result (Fig. 4C). The insets of Fig. 4C are the magnified lattice fringes of the chosen area in Fig. 4C. The clear lattice fringe appeared on the CdS NR with an interplanar spacing of 0.334 nm, attributed to the highly crystalline CdS (002) plane. The lattice fringes corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of Pd nanoparticles with an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm and the (102) crystal plane of CQDs with an interplanar spacing of 0.149 nm were also clearly observed on the CdS NR, indicating the close contact of the CdS NR with Pd nanoparticles and CQDs at the nanoscale [47,48]. This result confirms successful synthesis of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite. The well distributed Pd and CQDs on the surface of CdS NRs may provide more active sites and form heterojunctions and Schottky junctions, beneficial to enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. To further confirm successful synthesis of the CQD dispersion, the TEM and HRTEM images of the as-prepared pure CQDs dispersion were taken, as seen in Fig. S1 of the ESI. The CQDs are uniformly dispersed with particle sizes around 4 nm, consistent with the result in Fig. 4. The clear lattice fringes were observed with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.149 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (102) crystal planes, respectively, of the CQDs [48].

XPS spectra were measured to reveal the element compositions and valence states of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS and CdS samples. The Cd 3d XPS spectrum of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite (Fig. 5 a) exhibits two distinct peaks with the binding energies of 411.7 eV (Cd 3d3/2) and 404.9 eV (Cd 3d5/2), originating from Cd2+ in the CdS NRs [46]. For the S 2p spectrum of this composite, two peaks are observed at 160.9 eV and 161.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b), corresponding to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 of S2− in the CdS [46]. It is noteworthy to mention that the binding energies for Cd 3d and S 2p in the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS shift toward the higher binding energies, compared with the CdS, suggesting that CdS acts as an electron donor in the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite, namely electrons flowing out from the CdS to Pd and CQDs.

The Pd 3d XPS spectrum of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite shows two distinct peaks at 341.0 eV and 335.7 eV, attributed to Pd 3d3/2 and Pd 3d5/2 of a metallic Pd [28], confirming complete conversion of Pd2+ to Pd0 on the CdS surface, which is in favor of photocatalytic reactions. In the C1s spectra, three peaks are observed for the two samples at the binding energies of 284.8 eV (C–C), 285.5 eV (C–S and C–O) and 288.6 eV (CO) [48]. Notably, the peak area ratio at 285.5 eV in the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite is relatively higher than that of the pure CdS, indicating the presence of strong interaction between the CQDs and CdS (C–S bonds), further confirming the successful loading of CQDs on the CdS surface.

Light absorption capability and separation degree of the photogenerated electron-hole on the catalyst surface were analyzed by UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra and solid photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 6 ). The pure CdS sample exhibits strong light absorption capability in the region of 300–520 nm with a band gap of 2.26 eV (Fig. 6a and Fig. S2 in the ESI). The most visible light in the range of 520–800 nm can't be well utilized for photocatalytic reactions. Loading CQDs on the CdS only slightly enhanced light absorption in the visible region. While the Pd loading distinctly intensified the visible light absorption. Upon loading the dual co-catalysts Pd and CQDs, the visible light absorption capabilities of the ternary composite samples were further significantly improved, accompanying with slight red shifts at the absorption band edges. Especially, the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS sample exhibits the strongest visible light absorption and the largest red shift at the absorption band edge, corresponding to the smallest band gap of 1.95 eV. The synergistic effect of Pd and CQDs results in much enhanced visible light absorption and narrowed band gap of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite, beneficial to photocatalytic reactions.

Charge separations on the surfaces of the series of samples can get clue from the PL spectra (Fig. 6b). The results show that the pure CdS exhibits the strongest fluorescence emission peak at 557 nm, indicating the highest recombination rate of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+), corresponding to the worst charge separation. For the CQDs or/and Pd loaded CdS composites, the PL peak intensities were significantly reduced, indicating that the loading of Pd and CQDs effectively inhibits recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes on the CdS surface. Particularly, the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS sample exhibits the lowest fluorescence intensity, indicating the least recombination of electrons and holes, corresponding to the best charge separation.

The transient photocurrent curves and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the sample coated electrodes were tested to investigate the charge transfer capabilities of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 6c, under visible light irradiation, the photocurrent values of all the samples rapidly increase and follow the order of Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS > Pd-0.5CQDs/CdS > Pd-0.2CQDs/CdS > Pd/CdS >0.35CQDs/CdS > CdS. The photocurrent density of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS is the highest, indicating that the introduction of an appropriate amount of CQDs and Pd nanoparticles can significantly enhance the charge transfer efficiency on the catalyst. The EIS results (Fig. 6d) display that the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS coated electrode possesses the smallest Nyquist radius, indicating the lowest charge transfer resistance, which is consistent with the photocurrent result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET surface areas of the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the fact that photocatalytic reactions occur on the surface of the catalyst, the specific surface area of a catalyst could be crucial to its activity. Therefore, the BET surface areas of the CdS NRs and the composite materials were measured by means of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. S3 in the ESI). The specific surface areas of the pure CdS, 0.35CQDs/CdS, Pd/CdS, Pd-0.2CQDs/CdS, Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS and Pd-0.5CQDs/CdS were 45.9, 39.3, 36.2, 34.5, 28.5 and 26.8 m2 g−1, respectively. Noticably the specific surface areas of the composites decrease apparently with loading of Pd and CQDs, which may be attributed to partial aggregation during the synthesis processes. The reduced BET surface areas of the composites are detrimental to the photocatalytic reactions.

In order to optimize the photocatalytic hydrogen production performances of the CdS-based ternary composite catalysts, a systematic investigation was conducted by tuning the contents of Pd and CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . When 0.35 mL of CQDs was used for synthesis of the ternary composites and increasing the Pd contents from 0.5% to 1.5%, the photocatalytic HERs of the as-obtained samples firstly increased from 15.9 (0.5%Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS) to 47.1 mmol h−1 g−1 (1.0%Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS) and then decreased to 28.6 mmol h−1 g−1 (1.5%Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS). Thus, the optimal content of Pd was regarded as 1.0%. When the Pd content was held at 1.0%, gradually increasing the CQDs volumes from 0 to 2.0 mL led to firstly progressive rise in HERs, reaching maximum at 0.35 mL, and then gradually decline. Thus, 0.35 mL of CQDs was treated as the optimal amount for synthesis of the ternary composite. Excess CQDs or Pd nanoparticles may occupy more active sites on the surface of CdS, and consequently diminish its activity. Subsequently, the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite with optimum quantities of CQDs (0.35 mL) and Pd (1.0%) was prepared for further study.

To understand the roles of Pd and CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of the ternary composite Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS, its visible light (>420 nm) driven H2 production capability was compared with those of the pure CdS, 0.35CQDs/CdS and Pd/CdS. As shown in Fig. 8 , the hydrogen production over the pure CdS was the lowest with the average HER of 1.3 mmol h−1 g−1. With introducing the co-catalyst CQDs, only slight improvement in average HER (1.8 mmol h−1 g−1) was observed for the catalyst 0.35CQDs/CdS. For the Pd loaded CdS catalyst, however, the average HER was dramatically increased to 18.5 mmol h−1 g−1. This enhancement can be attributed to the formation of Schottky junctions between the CdS NRs and Pd nanoparticles, which greatly accelerated migration of photogenerated electrons and improved separation degree of electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the Pd nanoparticles can provide extra active sites for H+ reduction to generate H2. When 1.0% of Pd and 0.35 mL of CQDs were co-deposited on the surface of CdS NRs, the as-synthesized ternary composite Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS exhibited much enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance with the average HER as high as 47.1 mmol h−1 g−1, which is 36.2, 26.2 and 2.5 times higher than those of the pure CdS, 0.35CQDs/CdS and Pd/CdS catalysts, respectively. This high HER can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the dual co-catalysts Pd and CQDs with a synergistic factor (η) of 2.32 (η = r
Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS /(r
0.35CQDs/CdS + r
Pd/CdS ), where r represents HER). Inevitably, the formation of heterojunctions between CdS and CQDs, together with Schottky junctions at the CdS–Pd and CQD-Pd interfaces significantly promoted charge transfer and separation, resulting in the excellent H2 production activity.

The apparent quantum efficiencies (AQEs) of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS catalyst were measured under two monochromatic lights (450 nm and 550 nm) irradiation, which are 17.1% and 9.7%, respectively. These results indicate that 450 nm of visible light is in favor of the photocatalytic H2 production reactions, which may be attributed to the stronger light absorption at 450 nm than at the longer wavelength 550 nm, as seen in Fig. 6a.

Compared with the reported works in Table 1 , the as-prepared sample Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS exhibits much higher visible-light catalytic hydrogen production efficiency. Under similar reaction conditions, the average photocatalytic HER of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS is 1.4–54 times those of the MoS2/CdS, NiS/CdS, Pt–PdS/CdS, MnOx@CdS/CoP, Ag2S/CdS–Pt, Cd/CdS/4%Pt and Pt–Pd/CdS catalysts [49–55], even for the Pt containing composites. Generally, Pt containing catalysts were believed possessing the higher photocatalytic H2 production activities due to the high work function of Pt. But Pt is too expensive, which limited its practical applications. In comparison, Pd is much cheaper than Pt, which gives the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite a big advantage in cost over the Pt containing catalysts. The other apparent advantage of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS catalyst is its superior visible light driven HER.

The photocatalytic stability of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite was studied, which was shown in Fig. S4 of the ESI. After four cycles (16 h) of photocatalytic reactions, the hydrogen production over the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite was decreased to 49.3% (Fig. S4a of the ESI), implying an unsatisfying cycling stability. The long-term stability test of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite under continuous visible light irradiation (Fig. S4b of the ESI) demonstrates that H2 gas was continuously produced for 32 h with a gradually declined HER. To investigate the influencing factors to the inactivation of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite, the used catalyst was reclaimed and characterized by XRD, SEM and XPS spectra, as illustrated in Fig. S5 of the ESI. The XRD results demonstrate a distinct decrease in diffraction peak intensity for the used catalyst, especially for the (101) crystal plane, indicating structural damage to the CdS NRs during the photocatalytic reactions and the (101) plane could be the most active crystal plane. The SEM results reveal that the CdS rod-like structure was partly cracked after the photocatalytic reactions. The XPS spectra show that both the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 peaks slightly shift toward the higher binding energies, compared to those of the fresh catalyst, indicating some electrons flowing out from the S2− after the photocatalytic reactions. But no element S peak was present. These results suggest that slight photocorrosion may occur on the CdS NRs during the photocatalytic reactions, which led to slight changes in crystal structure and morphology of the catalyst material. Both the lowered crystallinity and partly broken rod-like structure of the CdS are not in favor of electron transfer along the surface of CdS nanorods to Pd and CQDs, and thereby the HER was decreased. To mitigate the activity drop, other semiconductor, for instance MoS2, could be used to stabilize the CdS nanorods based on the photocorrosion-recrystallization strategy [27].

The CdS is highly toxic and not environmentally friendly catalyst. Thus, the CdS composite catalyst must be reclaimed carefully after the photocatalytic reactions. Fortunately, CdS is an insoluble solid with a low K
sp constant (3.6 × 10−29). Therefore the solubility of the CdS or the concentration of the leaching Cd2+ ions in the reaction medium is about 6 × 10−15 M, which is much lower than the WHO criteria for Cd2+ in drinking water, 2.67 × 10−8 M (or 3 μg L−1 [56]), meeting environmental criteria. Although mild photocorrosion of the CdS catalyst may cause slight Cd2+ leaching into the aqueous solution due to S2− (from CdS) oxidation by the holes or hydroxyl radicals [16], the S2− (from the sacrificial agent Na2S) would combine with Cd2+, forming CdS precipitate, avoiding pollution by Cd2+ ions. Thus, the Na2S–Na2SO3 solution is a suitable sacrificial agent for H2 generation system over the CdS-based photocatalyst.

Since the energy band structure of a semiconductor catalyst could influence on its photocatalytic activity [15], the conduction band potentials (E
CB ) and valence band potentials (E
VB ) of the series of CdS-based samples were obtained by means of Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. Fig. S6 presents the M − S plots of the CdS-based series of samples coated electrodes. The results demonstrate that all samples exhibit positive slopes, confirming that they are all n-type semiconductors. Typically, the flat band potential (V
fb ) of a n-type semiconductor is 0.1–0.3 eV higher than the E
CB value [57]. Thus, the formula E
CB = V
fb - 0.3 eV was used to calculate the E
CB values. The E
VB values can then be derived using the formula E
g = E
VB - E
CB , where the band gap energies (E
g ) were obtained from the Tauc plots (Fig. S2 in ESI) based on the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra in Fig. 6a. The energy band structures of the series of samples were then plotted according to these values, as presented in Scheme S2 of ESI. The E
CB of the pure CdS NRs was found to be −0.63 eV (vs. NHE). Upon loading the CQDs or/and Pd, the E
CB values of the composites shift positively and E
VB values shift negatively, which are likely due to the formation of Schottky junctions at the Pd–CdS and Pd-CQD interfaces, together with the type I heterojunction between CdS and CQDs, given that the Fermi levels of Pd and CQDs are significantly lower than that of the CdS NRs. The Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS photocatalyst exhibits the maximum shifts in CB and VB potentials, resulting in the most narrowed band gap, which may excite the most electrons for H+ reduction reactions, leading to the highest HER.

Based on the energy band structure diagrams of the CdS and the series of composite samples (Scheme S2 in the ESI), a hypothesis for the photocatalytic hydrogen production mechanism over the ternary catalyst Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS was proposed (Scheme 2 ). Under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), electrons in the CdS of the ternary composite are excited and transit to its conduction band, while the holes remain in the valence band of CdS. Part of electrons in the conduction band of CdS directly reduce H+ to produce H2. The other part of electrons will rapidly migrate to the conduction band of CQDs and finally to the Pd nanoparticles because their work functions follow the sequence of EF (CdS, 4.17 eV, obtained from the M − S plot in Fig. S6a of the ESI) < EF (CQDs, 4.99 eV) < EF (Pd, 5.6 eV) [20]. This can also get clue from the XPS results (Fig. 5), where the binding energies of Cd 3d and S 2p in the ternary composite slightly shift towards the higher values, compared to those of the pure CdS, indicating an electron-donating state of the CdS NRs under illumination. As a result, large number of electrons accumulate on the Pd nanoparticles and CQDs, where the reductions of H+ occur by capturing electrons from them to produce H2. In this process, the Schottky junctions formed at the Pd–CdS and Pd-CQD interfaces accelerate electron transfer towards the Pd nanoparticles and hinder electrons flowing back to the semiconductors CdS and CQDs. Meanwhile the type I heterojunctions were formed at the CdS-CQD interfaces. The combined action of Schottky junctions and type I heterojunctions (or synergistic effect of CQDs and Pd) led to the most efficient electron transfer and the best charge separation on the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS catalyst, ultimately resulting in the highest photocatalytic H2 production activity. In addition, the introduction of Pd and CQDs also intensified visible light absorption and narrowed the band gap of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS, improving utilization on visible light, which may also partly contribute to the enhanced activity. The loading of CQDs or/and Pd resulted in decrease in BET surface areas of the composites, conflicting with their enhanced activities. Thus, in the present work, the BET surface area of the catalyst is not a decisive factor to the photocatalytic activity.

It is worthy to mention that the photocatalytic HER for the binary composite 0.35CQDs/CdS was only slightly increased, compared to the pure CdS catalyst, indicating that the individual type I heterojunctions merely improved the electron-hole separation to limited extent. Although the as-formed type I heterojunction accelerates the electron transfer from CdS to CQD and therefore postpones the electron-hole recombination to certain extent, the electrons on the CB of CQDs are much easier to recombine with the holes in its VB due to its rather narrow band gap. For the ternary composite Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS, however, the combined action of the Schottky junctions (at Pd-CQD interfaces) and type I heterojunctions (at CdS-CQD interfaces) greatly accelerate electron migration form CdS to CQD and finally to Pd, while the holes mainly remain in the VB of CQD, significantly depressing the recombination of electrons and holes. Thus, the charge transfer efficiency and separation degree in the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite was greatly promoted, leading to much enhanced photocatalytic HER. The HER value for the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite (47.1 mmol h−1 g−1) is much higher than the sum total of the Pd/CdS (18.5 mmol h−1 g−1) and 0.35CQDs/CdS (1.8 mmol h−1 g−1), indicating synergistic effect of Pd and CQDs to the CdS NRs.

In summary, Pd nanoparticles and CQDs were successfully modified on the surface of CdS NRs, forming the Pd-CQDs/CdS ternary composites which exhibited much enhanced visible light (λ > 420 nm) catalytic H2 production activities. Especially, when the optimal 100 μL of PdCl2 and 0.35 mL of CQD dispersion were used for synthesis, the as-obtained Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite displayed the highest photocatalytic HER, 47.1 mmol h−1 g−1, which is 36.2, 26.2 and 2.5 times higher than that of the pure CdS, the binary catalyst 0.35CQDs/CdS and Pd/CdS, respectively. The apparent quantum efficiencies of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite catalyst under two monochromatic lights (450 nm and 550 nm) irradiation were 17.1% and 9.7%, respectively. The formation of Schottky junctions at both the Pd–CdS and Pd-CQD interfaces, together with type I heterojunctions formed between CdS NRs and CQDs greatly promoted charge transfer efficiency and separation degree, resulting in much enhanced visible light H2 production activity of the Pd-0.35CQDs/CdS composite catalyst. The synergistic effect of the Pd and CQDs also increased visible light utilization and optimized the energy band structure of the catalyst, contributing to enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. This work provides a simple and cost-effective strategy to enhance the visible-light catalytic H2 production performance of CdS, thereby increasing the practical application potential of the photocatalysts. In the future work, Pd clusters or single atoms would be considered as the co-catalyst of CdS to further enhance the H2 production performance. The CdS could be partly capped by other semiconductors, such as MoS2, to reduce photocorrosion, and consequently increase the stability.
